claimed_contribution: Introduces the space-to-product ratio effect: allocating more interstitial space to a fixed assortment improves consumer responses.
Identifies two distinct mechanisms, with higher space increasing perceived product aesthetics and perceived store prestige, and shows these mechanisms differ in processing demands.
Shows practical and theoretical relevance by documenting effects on field sales, purchase intentions, valuation, and perceived consumption experience (taste) across multiple categories and settings.
context: The paper studies physical product displays in retail-like environments, including a real jewelry stand and a real chocolate sampling stand, plus controlled display images in lab-style experiments. Settings span jewelry, personal care products, earrings, and chocolates, with both hedonic and utilitarian items and varying brand familiarity.
design_and_data: The evidence comes from four studies combining field and laboratory-style experiments. Study 1 is a field experiment at a Lexi Jewelry stand over two days, holding 173 items and arrangement constant while doubling table size; it tracks visitors and purchases (197 visitors) and collects exit surveys (n = 86). Study 2 is a 2 x 2 x 2 between-subjects online experiment with 512 MTurk participants manipulating space-to-product ratio, cognitive load, and product type (hedonic vs utilitarian), with measures of fair price, purchase likelihood, perceived aesthetics, and store prestige. Study 3 is a 2 x 3 between-subjects experiment with 401 undergraduates manipulating space and brand context (Tiffany & Co., Forever 21, no brand), measuring fair price, willingness to pay, purchase likelihood, and the proposed mediators; Study 4 is a field chocolate taste test (8 hours, space condition alternated every 2 hours) with 166 participants from 458 approaches, measuring taste and mediators.
main_findings: Increasing interstitial space while holding assortment and organization constant increased product evaluations, purchase likelihood, and per-visitor spending in the field jewelry setting.

Across controlled studies, the effect of space on valuation and purchase was mediated by perceived product aesthetics and perceived store prestige; under high cognitive load and with strong brand cues, the store-prestige pathway weakened while the aesthetics pathway persisted.

In a field chocolate test, higher space-to-product ratio increased perceived taste, and this effect was mediated by perceived product aesthetics and perceived store prestige; multiple alternatives (e.g., scarcity, popularity, messiness, search difficulty) were not supported.
one_line_summary: Across field and lab studies, increasing interstitial display space for a fixed assortment raises valuation, purchase behavior, and even perceived taste through higher perceived product aesthetics and store prestige.
relation_to_prior_work: introduces a display-space effect that prior shelf-space and assortment work largely did not test because those studies typically varied item quantity, variety, or organization while keeping display space fixed. It also differs from white-space advertising research by examining in-store interstitial space around constant assortments and by documenting distinct prestige and aesthetics pathways.
research_gap: Prior retail research had mainly examined quantity, variety, and organization effects under fixed display space, leaving unclear whether space around a constant set of products changes consumer judgments and behavior. White-space findings from artistic advertising were not directly applicable to in-store product displays, motivating a retail-specific test and mechanism account.
research_question: Does increasing the space-to-product ratio in retail displays, while holding product assortment and organization constant, improve consumer valuation and choice outcomes? Through which mechanisms does this occur - perceived store prestige and/or perceived product aesthetics - and when does each pathway attenuate (e.g., under cognitive load or strong brand cues)?
